text: Patients presenting with chest pain suggestive of acute coronary syndrome should receive an ECG within 10 minutes of arrival and troponin testing per institutional protocol. Risk stratification follows the HEART score; admit pathways are defined for high-risk strata.
STEMI activation requires ST-segment elevation ≥1 mm in two contiguous leads (≥2 mm in V2–V3) on ECG. Door-to-balloon time goal is 90 minutes for percutaneous coronary intervention.

Suspected acute ischemic stroke with onset within 4.5 hours warrants emergent non-contrast head CT and consideration of IV thrombolysis if no contraindications. Large-vessel occlusion may require endovascular thrombectomy up to 24 hours from last known well.

Thunderclap headache reaching peak intensity within seconds requires emergent non-contrast head CT. If CT is non-diagnostic and the clinical suspicion remains high, lumbar puncture is indicated to evaluate for subarachnoid hemorrhage.

Anaphylaxis with respiratory or circulatory involvement requires intramuscular epinephrine in the anterolateral thigh as first-line treatment, with rapid reassessment. Antihistamines and corticosteroids are adjuncts, not substitutes.

Third-trimester vaginal bleeding requires immediate obstetric evaluation. Placenta previa and abruption are leading concerns; digital vaginal examination is contraindicated until placental location is known by ultrasound.
